statement.markdown:
Given an integer `n`, return `true` if it's possible to place `n` queens on an `n x n` chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. The task is incomplete and has a logical bug in it's implementation. Complete the code to make it pass all test cases.

Example inputs and their expected outputs:

- `n = 1` => `true`
- `n = 2` => `false`
- `n = 3` => `false`
statement.inputFormat: An integer `n` representing the size of the chessboard.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether it's possible to place `n` queens on the board such that no two queens attack each other.
statement.notes: You can use a simple backtracking approach to solve this problem. Consider using a recursive DFS function and a visited set to track the positions of placed queens.

examples.explanation: One queen can be placed on the board.
Two queens cannot be placed on the board without attacking each other.
Three queens cannot be placed on the board without attacking each other.

hints.text: Ensure that your backtracking function considers all possible positions for the next queen.

editorial.title: Solving N-Queens with Backtracking
editorial.intuition: Use a recursive DFS function to place queens on the board, considering all possible positions for each queen. Use a visited set to track the positions of placed queens.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach involves iterating over all possible board configurations and checking if they satisfy the conditions.
editorial.betterSolution: Using backtracking with a visited set and considering all possible positions for each queen is a more efficient approach.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses backtracking with a visited set and considers all possible positions for each queen, with a time complexity of O(n!).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution is based on the fact that it considers all possible board configurations and checks if they satisfy the conditions.
editorial.dryRun: Start by placing the first queen on the board and then recursively place the remaining queens, considering all possible positions for each queen.
commonMistakes: Not considering all possible positions for each queen
Not using a visited set to track the positions of placed queens
